1:
The reign opens with the annalist's spare formula — a date, a length, a mother's name — and the witnesses unfold from these dry notices the whole shape of the story to come. The Reformed Gill works the synchronism: Jehoash began in the seventh year of Jehu, and since Jehu reigned twenty-eight years, the two kings ran side by side for some twenty-one or twenty-two of them. The grammatical-historical Ellicott studies the habits of the record itself: 'forty years' is 'a common round number,' for David and Solomon are each said to have reigned forty; the author of these short abstracts generally gives the mother's name in regard to the kings of Judah; and Zibiah's town of Beersheba was 'a famous Simeonite sanctuary, and resort of pilgrims' (Amos 5:5; Amos 8:14).

Keil, of the same school, surveys what the forty years will contain: beyond the general character of the reign, only the repairing of the temple, the purchased retreat of the Syrians from their invasion of Judah, and finally the king's violent death at the hands of conspirators. The parallel account in 2 Chronicles 24 supplies several additions — the wives of Joash, the distribution of the Levites at the repairing of the temple, the death of Jehoiada, the seduction of Joash to idolatry by the chief men of Judah, and the stoning of the prophet Zechariah who condemned that rebellion — 'all of which,' he judges, 'can easily be fitted into our account.' The Wesleyan Clarke draws the same outline in a sentence: Jehoash reigns well under the instructions of Jehoiada the priest, directs the repairing of the temple, gives Hazael the treasures and hallowed things of the house of the Lord to turn him back from Jerusalem, and falls at last by the conspiracy of his own servants.

The puritan Matthew Henry attends to the man rather than the outline. Joash reigned forty years, but since he began so very young he 'might, in the course of nature, have continued much longer, for he was cut off when he was but forty-seven years old.' What kept him right, so long as he was kept, was not his own goodness but his guardian: it is 'a great mercy to young people, and especially to young princes,' to be under good direction and to have about them those who will instruct them to do what is right in the sight of the Lord; and Joash showed a wisdom of his own in being 'willing to be counselled and ruled' even after he was grown. So the witnesses set the reader at the head of the chapter with open eyes: a boy crowned out of hiding, a long reign whose one monument is a repaired temple, and — as Keil and Clarke both report from Chronicles — a shadow already falling across the dates, for when the good priest died, the goodness of the reign died with him.
2:
The verdict on Joash is given with one hand and measured with the other, and the witnesses fasten on the clause that limits it. The Reformed Gill states the substance simply: Jehoash did that which was right in the sight of the Lord, 'worshipping the only true God, and ruling and walking according to the law of God.' But everything turns on the words 'all his days wherein Jehoiada the priest instructed him,' and here the grammatical-historical commentators do their closest work. Keil argues that the Hebrew means 'all his days that Jehoiada instructed him,' not — though the accent under the word rather favours that view — 'all his life long, because Jehoiada had instructed him'; for Jehoiada did not teach the king before he came to the throne, but after he was set upon it at seven years old, that is, for as long as Jehoiada himself lived. The reading of Chronicles, 'all the days of Jehoiada,' is therefore 'a correct explanation.' Ellicott agrees that the Hebrew is ambiguous but 'may certainly mean this,' as the Septuagint and Vulgate render it; and he conjectures how the sentence came by its peculiar form — the writer first set down the usual statement concerning kings who supported the worship of Jehovah, and then, remembering the evils which ensued upon the high priest's death (2 Chron. 24:17), added the qualifying clause as a correction.

Behind the grammar stands a real quarrel. Thenius insisted that the words can only be rendered 'all his life long, because Jehoiada had instructed him' — a lifelong righteousness that would set this verse against the chronicler's account of the apostasy, so that the ambiguity 'gave an opportunity for discrediting the chronicler.' Ellicott will not allow it: the words certainly can be rendered as our version renders them, and even, he adds, as 'whom Jehoiada the priest instructed.' The Wesleyan Clarke reads the verse as the chronicler's history requires: while Jehoiada, 'a pious, holy man,' lived, Jehoash walked uprightly; but it appears from 2 Chronicles 24:17-18 that after the death of this eminent high priest he departed from the worship of the true God, lapsed into idolatry, and 'seems to have had a share in the murder of Zechariah,' who testified against his transgressions and those of the princes of Judah. Keil traces the same descent: after Jehoiada's death Joash yielded to the petitions of the princes of Judah that he would assent to their worshipping idols, and at length went so far as to stone the son of his benefactor.

Clarke closes with the sigh that gives the verse its pastoral weight: 'O how few of the few who begin to live to God continue unto the end!' The praise of Joash is real, but it is borrowed light; his righteousness had a keeper, and the witnesses agree that when the keeper was gone the righteousness went with him. What reads at first like the commendation of a life is, on the commentators' showing, the epitaph of a goodness that lasted exactly as long as its instructor.
3:
One reservation stands against the fair record: the high places were not taken away. The grammatical-historical Keil marks the clause as the standing formula of these books (compare 1 Kings 15:14) — the worship on the heights 'was not entirely suppressed, notwithstanding the fact that Jehoiada instructed him.' Ellicott renders the opening word 'save that,' as at 2 Kings 15:4, and points the verbs: the people 'were wont to sacrifice' and burn incense there still; the worship of the high places 'continued even under the régime of Jehoiada.' The Reformed Gill explains the tenacity of the custom: these altars had been used before the temple was built, and were set up again in Rehoboam's time contrary to the law which fixed sacrifice at the one place the Lord chose to dwell in; the people had clung to them through all preceding reigns, as they had in the days of Athaliah, and it was not in the power of the best of kings — or at least they did not think it safe to attempt it — to remove them before Hezekiah's time; 'so fond were the people of them because of their antiquity and supposed sanctity, and for the sake of ease.'

The puritan Matthew Henry pleads what can be pleaded. The country altars were 'to the honour of the God of Israel only,' though in competition with, and at least in tacit contempt of, his altar at Jerusalem. Perhaps they had been more used in the late bad reigns than formerly, because it was not safe to go up to Jerusalem and the temple-service was not performed as it should have been; and it may be that Jehoiada connived at them, because some well-meaning people were glad of them when they could not have better, and he hoped that the reforming of the temple would by degrees draw people from their high places and they would dwindle of themselves; or perhaps neither the king nor the priest had zeal enough to carry the reformation so far, nor courage and strength enough to encounter so inveterate a usage.

The Wesleyan Clarke will have none of these extenuations. 'Without the total destruction of these there could be no radical reform.' The toleration of any species of idolatry in the land, whatever else was done in behalf of true religion, 'left, and in effect fostered, a seed which springing up, regenerated in time the whole infernal system.' And he lays the failure squarely at the door of the man best placed to prevent it: 'Jehoiada did not use his influence as he might have done; for as he had the king's heart and hand with him, he might have done what he pleased.' So the witnesses genuinely divide — Henry excusing what Clarke indicts, and Gill counting the thing scarcely possible — while agreeing on the fact: the reformation of Joash restored the temple but never touched the hills, and the seed left growing there would outlive both the king and his priest.
4:
With this verse the chapter's great work begins, and the witnesses ask first why this king above all others should be the temple's restorer. The Reformed Gill answers out of the story itself: Joash had it in his heart to repair the house 'not only because it was the sanctuary of the Lord, though that chiefly, but because it had been a sanctuary to him, where he was hid and preserved six years.' The Wesleyan Wesley says the same — remembering that he owed his preservation and his restoration to the temple, and that God had made him the guardian of his temple, he now takes care to repair it — and adds a shrewd note on the tense: 'that is brought' should rather be 'that shall be brought,' for little money is likely to have come in 'in the tyrannical and idolatrous reign of Athaliah.'

The commentators then sort the revenues the king assigns. The grammatical-historical Keil distinguishes three kinds of payment within the general 'money of the holy gifts': the money of persons mustered in the census, 'he who passes over to those who are numbered' (Exod. 30:13); the money of persons 'set at' a valuation, every kind of redemption-money; and all that comes into a man's heart to bring of his own accord. Clarke reckons the same three — the dedicated things, the free-will offerings, and the half-shekel paid from twenty years old 'for the redemption of their souls, that is their lives' (Exod. 30:12) — and observes tartly that all these sources 'had ever been in some measure open,' but that instead of repairing the Lord's house 'the priests and Levites had converted the income to their own use.' Ellicott, however, dissents on the middle phrase: 'the money of every one that passeth the account' means rather 'current money' (compare Gen. 23:16) — silver of full weight passing from hand to hand, for 'there was no such thing as a Hebrew coinage before the exile' — wanted so that it 'might be paid out immediately to the workpeople employed in the repairs.' He grants that the chronicler, who calls these revenues 'the tax of Moses' (2 Chron. 24:6), evidently understood chiefly the census half-shekel of Exodus 30:12-16; the valuation-money he refers to redemption of the first-born (Num. 18:16) and of vows, whose price the priest fixed (Lev. 27:2).

The puritan Matthew Henry watches the king rather than the ledger. The king himself was 'the first and forwardest man that took care for the repair'; and this for two reasons — because he was king, 'and God expects and requires from those who have power that they use it for the maintenance and support of religion'; and because the temple 'had been both his nursery and his sanctuary when he was a child, in a grateful remembrance of which he now appeared zealous for the honour of it.' Those who have experienced the comfort and benefit of religious assemblies, Henry concludes, will make the support of them their care and the prosperity of them their chief joy. So the verse opens the fund with a divided reading of one phrase — census-silver or current coin — and an undivided reading of the king's heart.
5:
The machinery of collection comes next, and the witnesses reconstruct it from the one odd word the verse turns on. The Reformed Gill pictures how the order would work: the priests had cities assigned them in several parts of the land, and those who dwelt in or near them 'were best known by them'; so they were sent into all the cities, 'some to one and some to another, where they were most acquainted,' to collect both what was due by law and what the people would freely give. The grammatical-historical Ellicott sets the two records side by side: from 2 Chron. 24:5 it is evident the chronicler understood the priests to be required to collect the moneys 'each in his own city and district, year by year'; our text taken alone would rather imply that persons coming to the temple — to have vows valued or to make free-will offerings — 'resorted to the priests whom they knew.' He notes as a curiosity that the word rendered 'acquaintance' occurs only in this account. Keil compresses the king's plan into its two clauses: the priests were to take the money, every man of his acquaintance, and with it repair the breaches of the house 'wheresoever any breach shall be found.'

On the breaches themselves Ellicott is exact: the dilapidations were serious 'not because of its age — it had only stood about 130 years — but owing to the wanton attacks of Athaliah and her sons' (2 Chron. 24:7), who had moreover diverted the revenues of the sanctuary to the support of the Baal-worship. The puritan Matthew Henry reads the same charge-sheet: it was not only the teeth of time that made these breaches; 'the sons of Athaliah had broken up the house of God,' and out of enmity to its service had damaged the buildings, while the priests 'had not taken care to repair the breaches in time, so that they went worse and worse.' 'Even temples themselves are the worse for the wearing,' he observes, 'but the heavenly temple will never wax old'; and he calls those husbandmen unworthy of their vineyard who could not keep the winepress in due and tenantable repair (Matt. 21:33).

From the phrase 'every man of his acquaintance' Henry draws a rule for common life. It was supposed 'that there was no man but had acquaintance with some or other of the priests'; and so, he writes, 'we should take the opportunity that God gives us of exciting those we have a particular acquaintance with to that which is good.' The design the witnesses describe is simple, personal, and — as the sequel will show — too gentle for the men it depended on. Collection and repair were left in the same priestly hands; the next verse counts what those hands accomplished.
6:
Twenty-three years into the reign the accounts are opened, and the breaches gape as wide as ever. The grammatical-historical Ellicott fixes the chronology: Jehoash may have ordered the restoration 'in his twentieth year, when he came of age,' so that the failure had run on for years under the king's own eye — and he finds it 'noticeable that he and not Jehoiada takes the initiative in the matter'; the chronicler states that the king had ordered the priests and the Levites 'to hasten the matter,' but that 'the Levites hastened it not' (2 Chron. 24:5). The Reformed Gill spreads out the possible faults without deciding among them: either the people were backward to pay in the money, or the priests converted it to their own use, or at the least they were negligent of the work enjoined them, 'either in collecting the money, or in using it as they were directed.'

The puritan Matthew Henry weighs the parties with his usual equity. Little money was raised: either the priests were careless and did not call on the people for their dues, or the people had so little confidence in the priests' management that they were backward to pay money into their hands — 'if they were distrusted without cause, it was the people's shame; if with, it was more theirs.' And what was raised was not applied: the priests thought the house 'might serve as it had done, and therefore put off repairing from time to time' — perhaps, he allows, because what little came in was needed for the priests' own maintenance, which must fall far short 'when ten tribes had wholly revolted and the other two were wretchedly corrupted.' His sentence on the whole affair has become proverbial: 'Church work is usually slow work, but it is a pity that churchmen, of all men, should be slow at it.'

The Wesleyan Clarke is severest. The orders had plainly been long given, and nothing was done 'merely through the inactivity and negligence of the priests'; the people had brought money in abundance, 'and the pious Jehoiada was over the priests, and yet nothing was done!' Jehoiada was a good man, but he 'does not appear to have had much of the spirit of an active zeal; and simple piety, without zeal and activity, is of little use when a reformation in religion and manners is necessary to be brought about.' Then follows his famous excursus: Melancthon was orthodox, pious, and learned, but a man of comparative inactivity; Luther was in many respects by far his inferior, 'but in zeal and activity he was a flaming and consuming fire,' and by him, under God, the mighty Reformation was effected — 'Ten thousand Jehoiadas and Melancthons might have wished it in vain; Luther worked, and God worked by him, in him, and for him.' So the witnesses divide over where the fault chiefly lay — priestly negligence, popular distrust, or a good man's want of zeal — while the fact stands against them all: for years the money came and went, and the house of God still stood in its breaches.
7:
The king calls his own tutor to account, and the witnesses watch the scene with a mixture of admiration and perplexity. The Reformed Gill notes the summons took in 'Jehoiada the priest, and the other priests' — the high priest together with the common priests under him. The grammatical-historical Keil explains what the king's question and order effected: since the money had been gathered and the breaches not repaired, Joash 'took away from them both the collection of the money and the obligation to repair the temple' — and the priests' consent in the next verse shows that this is how his command is to be understood. Ellicott owns that 'the account of the whole transaction is not very clear, and commentators disagree upon the question of the degree of blame attaching to the priests.' His own reconstruction is measured: the revenues of the sanctuary had probably been 'in a very languishing condition during the late reigns,' the priesthood had used whatever offerings they received for their own support, and they 'would now very naturally be unwilling' to give over to the work of repair revenues 'which they had come to regard as their own'; indeed, from the account in Chronicles 'it would not appear that any money was collected for the purpose of restoration before the king took the matter into his own hands.' He rejects the idea of Thenius that Joash wished 'to humble the pride of the priests by diminishing their revenues' — it 'is not contained in either narrative' — yet grants two things: that the moral tone of the whole order had likely degenerated in the late period of apostasy, and that the king's closing words, rightly rendered 'for to the dilapidation of the house ye should give it,' 'certainly seem to throw suspicion on the priests.'

The puritan Matthew Henry admires the king's part. His heart was 'much set upon having the breaches of the house repaired'; we commend his zeal, 'and blame him not for reproving even his tutor Jehoiada himself when he saw him remiss'; and so convincing was the reproof 'that the priests owned themselves unworthy to be any longer employed' in the business. Yet Henry cannot forbear a glance at the end of the story: the king's later apostasy 'gives us cause to question whether he had as good an affection for the service of the temple as he had for the structure. Many have been zealous for building and beautifying churches, and for other forms of godliness, who yet have been strangers to the power of it.'

So the verse leaves the priests suspected but not convicted, and the witnesses are content to leave them so — Ellicott admitting the suspicion, Keil (as he will argue at v. 11) denying the crime, Henry praising the reprover while questioning his depth. The fund changes hands; the work, at last, is about to change pace.
8:
The priests' answer is a single clause — they consented — and the witnesses read a small history in it. The Reformed Gill fills out the plain sense: they 'consented to receive no more money of the people,' and, he adds, 'delivered up what they had.' The grammatical-historical Ellicott supposes the consent was not given in silence: 'no doubt they made some such explanation' as would clear themselves 'from the suspicion of fraud; after which, they agreed to resign all connection with the business.' Keil draws from the consent the key to the whole transaction: what the king proposed and the priests accepted was that both the collecting of the money and the charge of the repairs should pass out of their hands — 'we may see from this consent how the command of the king is to be understood.' Hitherto the priests had gathered the funds and stood bound to execute the work; henceforth they would do neither. And the very fact that their consent was asked, he argues later in the chapter, tells decisively against the notion that they were suspected thieves: a king who believed his priests embezzlers would not have negotiated with them.

The puritan Matthew Henry turns the resignation into its own kind of praise. The king's reproof was so convincing that the priests 'owned themselves unworthy to be any longer employed, and consented to the taking of some other measures, and the giving up of the money they had received into other hands.' And then the sentence that redeems them: 'It was honestly done, when they found they had not spirit to do it themselves, not to hinder other people from doing it.'

The witnesses thus colour the same moment differently — an answer to suspicion in Ellicott, an orderly redistribution of duties in Keil, an honest surrender in Henry — but they agree entirely on what it accomplished. The men who could not build stood aside for men who could, and the reform of God's house, which had waited on clerical energy for the better part of a reign, waited no longer. There is a quiet lesson here which Henry states and the others imply: in the work of God it is no shame to lay down an office one cannot fill, and the truest service a weary or unfit hand can render is to make way. The priests' consent, whatever mixture of embarrassment and relief lay behind it, opened the door through which the chest, the overseers, and the carpenters of the following verses now come.
9:
Out of the long failure comes one of the homeliest inventions in Scripture: a chest with a hole bored in its lid. The Reformed Gill notes that Jehoiada took it 'by the commandment of the king' (2 Chron. 24:8), and to a plain purpose — 'to prevent any fraud, or suspicion of any.' The grammatical-historical Ellicott reconstructs the working of the device: the chest was kept locked; the priests that kept the door — the Levitical doorkeepers — received the money from those who offered it and 'dropped it at once into the chest,' which 'obviated all suspicion of a possible misapplication of the contributions'; and the chronicler adds that offerings were asked 'by proclamation throughout the country,' and that the princes and people readily contributed.

Its position raises the chapter's next small debate. Kings sets the chest beside the altar, on the right side as one comes into the house of the Lord; Chronicles, 'in the gate of the house of the Lord outwards.' The Wesleyan Clarke harmonizes by a removal: the chest 'was at first set beside the altar, as is here mentioned; but afterwards, for the convenience of the people, it was set without the gate' (2 Chron. 24:8). Ellicott doubts the harmony: 'this can hardly refer to the same position'; the chronicler's notice probably describes where the chest — 'which became a permanent feature of the sanctuary' — stood in the time after the return from the Captivity.

The puritan Matthew Henry draws out the devotion hidden in the mechanics. The money was not paid into private hands but into a public chest, 'and then people brought it in readily and in great abundance' — 'when public distributions are made faithfully, public contributions will be made cheerfully.' Two details he will not let pass. The gift was dropped through the hole in the lid 'past recall, to intimate that what has been once resigned to God must never be resumed. Every man, as he purposeth in his heart, so let him give.' And the chest was put 'on the right hand as they went in,' in which some think there is an allusion in our Saviour's rule of charity, 'Let not thy left hand know what thy right hand doeth.' So the witnesses turn a carpenter's contrivance into a little treatise on giving: secure enough to kill suspicion, public enough to kindle cheerfulness, and final enough to teach that what passes through the slot has changed owners for ever.
10:
When the chest had done its silent work, the counting began, and the witnesses linger over the procedure as over a model of good administration. The Reformed Gill explains how 'they saw that there was much money in the chest': the sum 'might easily be guessed at by the number of the people which contributed.' The grammatical-historical Ellicott describes the routine — read 'And it came to pass': whenever the chest was full, the king's scribe and the high priest went up into the temple and emptied it. 'Put up in bags, and told' is literally 'they bound up and counted': they put the pieces of silver into bags of a certain size, then counted the bags, weighed them, and sealed them up, and the sealed bags 'would be paid out as money' just as they were (compare 2 Kings 5:23). Ewald, he notes, prefers the reading of Chronicles, 'they emptied,' which in Hebrew writing is very similar; and the royal secretary came 'as the king's representative, to make a record of the amount.'

The Wesleyan Clarke sees in the pairing of offices the lesson of the whole reform: 'It was necessary to associate with the high priest some civil authority and activity, in order to get the neglected work performed.' The puritan Matthew Henry describes the same scene from the treasury side: the high priest and the secretary of state 'counted the money out of the chest, and laid it by in specie for the use to which it was appropriated' — set apart, that is, beyond the reach of any second thoughts.

The verse rewards attention for the picture it preserves of a world before coinage — Ellicott has already observed on v. 4 that the currency of the age was silver of fixed weight, so that the weighed and sealed bag was itself the money — and for the double witness at the counting-table. Neither the crown's man nor the church's man handles the offerings alone; the scribe and the high priest stand over the open chest together, and the record made by the one is the security of the other. What Athaliah's sons had once broken up and plundered is now guarded by the simplest of devices: a locked box, a bored lid, a public emptying, and two pairs of honest eyes upon the silver.
11:
The silver now passes from the counting-table to the scaffold, and the witnesses attend closely to whose hands it touches. The grammatical-historical Keil renders with precision: 'They gave the money weighed into the hands of those who did the work, who were placed over the house of Jehovah' — that is, the appointed overlookers of the work — and these paid it out, as it was required, to the men upon the walls. Ellicott confirms both points: 'they used to give' it, each time the chest was emptied; and 'them that did the work' means here 'not the actual workmen, but... those who had the oversight of the house,' the superintendents. The Reformed Gill adds that these overseers 'were laymen, and not priests,' the priests being discharged from any concern in the repairs; and he preserves Kimchi's curiosity about the money 'told' — that coined and uncoined silver was brought, and what was not coined they coined before delivering it to the overseers to pay the workmen with.

Then Keil plants his flag. 'It is quite clear from this,' he writes, 'that the assertion of J. D. Michaelis, De Wette, and others, that the priests had embezzled the money collected, is perfectly imaginary. For if the king had cherished any such suspicion against the priests, he would not have asked for their consent to an alteration of the first arrangement or to the new measure.' The chapter's running question about the priests' guilt here receives its firmest negative — over against Clarke, who had charged them with converting the income to their own use, and Ellicott, who allowed that the king's words seem to throw suspicion upon them.

The puritan Matthew Henry approves the new channel on grounds of vocation. The money was not put into the hands of the priests, 'who were not versed in affairs of this nature, having other work to mind, but into the hands of those that did the work, or at least had the oversight of it.' 'Tractant fabrilia fabri — every artist has his trade assigned'; and let not those who are called to the holy warfare 'entangle themselves in the affairs of this life.' The arrangement the witnesses describe is thus both practical and principled: weighed money, lay stewards, priestly hands kept free for priestly work — and, on Keil's reading at least, an honourable discharge for the priests rather than a disgrace. The house begins to rise again precisely where the callings are at last kept distinct.
12:
The verse is a bill of trades, and the witnesses take its plainness seriously. The Reformed Gill glosses the workmen: the hewers cut the stone 'out of the mountain,' and with it the masons repaired the stone walls of the house. The grammatical-historical Ellicott attends to the small words: the Hebrew has 'the masons... the hewers,' with the article, as of standing bodies of craftsmen; 'hewed stone' is rather quarry stone; the tense of 'was laid out' implies that the paying 'was done repeatedly,' disbursement after disbursement as the chest was emptied; and the closing word for 'repair' does not recur in this sense elsewhere. Keil's rendering gathers the whole stream of expenditure into one sentence: the overseers paid it 'to the carpenters and builders, who worked at the house, and to the masons and hewers of stone, and for the purchase of wood and hewn stones, to repair the dilapidations of the house, and for all that might be spent for the house for repairing it.'

The puritan Matthew Henry marks how the entrusted men went about it: they did the business 'carefully, purchasing materials and paying workmen,' for 'business is done with expedition when those are employed in it that understand it and know which way to go about it.' The remark is less trivial than it looks; the first arrangement had failed for twenty-three years in the hands of men whose employment lay elsewhere, and the second succeeded at once in the hands of men whose employment lay exactly here.

There is a quiet dignity in the verse that the witnesses' patience brings out. The house of the Lord is not repaired by miracle but by carpenters and masons paid week by week out of a locked chest; the iterative tenses Ellicott observes show not one heroic payment but a standing administration, a fabric fund in continuous and faithful operation. The silver that pious Israel dropped through the bored lid becomes beams and quarry stone by way of honest wages; and the commentators, who quarrel elsewhere in the chapter over blame and custom, find nothing here to dispute. The work so long delayed is now simply being done — and Scripture, which can pass over decades in a clause, pauses to name every trade that had a hand in it, as if to teach that when God's house is at last being mended, the carpenter's day-wage is worth recording in the word of God.
13:
A restriction governed the fund, and the witnesses turn it over admiringly: none of the chest's silver went to plate. The Reformed Gill identifies the forfeited luxuries — the bowls of silver were vessels 'either to hold the blood of the sacrifices, or the drink offerings of wine.' The grammatical-historical Ellicott notes that the same three terms, bowls, snuffers, and basons, stand together in the inventory of Solomon's making (1 Kings 7:50); the trumpets are 'the straight priestly trumpets'; the tense is again continuous — 'there used not to be made' — the rule holding through the whole work; and 'the plain meaning is that the whole amount offered was expended on the necessary work of restoring the Temple fabric.'

But Chronicles tells of vessels of gold and silver made from this very restoration, and the Wesleyan Clarke supplies the harmony: 'there were no vessels made for the service of the temple till all the outward repairs were completed; but after this was done, they brought the rest of the money before the king and Jehoiada, whereof were made vessels of gold and silver' (2 Chron. 24:14). The puritan Matthew Henry reads it the same way and draws the household lesson that has made the verse famous: 'They did not lay it out in ornaments for the temple, in vessels of gold or silver, but in necessary repairs first,' whence 'we may learn, in all our expenses, to give that the preference which is most needful, and, in dealing for the public, to deal as we would for ourselves.' After the repairs were finished, he notes, 'we find the overplus turned into plate for the service of the temple' (2 Chron. 24:14).

The order of expenditure is the verse's theology. Ornament is not despised — the trumpets and basons will come, and Chronicles records their coming — but it must wait upon soundness: the roof before the silver, the wall before the wine-bowl. The witnesses hear in this the honest instinct of the whole reform, which had been provoked in the first place by sacred revenues sliding into comfortable uses; the new stewards will not repeat the old fault in a nobler form by beautifying a house that still stands broken. First the breaches, says the verse; and only when the breaches are stopped may the beauty begin.
14:
The positive side of the rule follows: the money went to the men who wrought. The Reformed Gill puts it in a sentence: 'They took care that the workmen should first be paid for the repairs of the temple.' The grammatical-historical Ellicott renders the clause literally — 'for to the doers of the work they used to give it, and they used to repair' — the iterative tense once more, payment following payment until the house was sound. Then comes his source-critical note. Chronicles adds that when the repairs were finished, the money left over was applied to making 'spoons and vessels of gold and silver' for the house of the Lord; and 'this certainly has the appearance of having been added to the original account, for the purpose of edifying the chronicler's contemporaries. He may, however, have found it in the compilation on which he mainly depended.' The Wesleyan Clarke, on the previous verse, had taken that addition at face value — no vessels till the outward repairs were done, then the residue brought before the king and Jehoiada (2 Chron. 24:14) — and the puritan Matthew Henry likewise finds the overplus honestly 'turned into plate for the service of the temple.' So a small but genuine debate attaches to the chronicler's sequel: to Clarke and Henry it is the natural second stage of the story; to Ellicott it wears the look of an edifying afterword, even if an honestly inherited one.

On the verse's own ground, however, the witnesses stand together. Wages come before ornaments; the fabric before the furniture. There is a scruple worth keeping in Gill's little word 'first': the labourer's hire is a debt, the ornament a devotion, and debts are paid before devotions are offered. The temple that the sons of Athaliah had broken up is restored not by splendour but by solvency — by a fund that paid its carpenters punctually and left the gold to wait for the surplus. And the quiet repetition of 'they gave... they repaired,' which Ellicott's grammar draws out, is itself the happy ending of the chapter's long first act: after decades in which money was received and nothing was done, every payment now issues in visible repair, and the giving and the mending run in one unbroken rhythm.
15:
The most remarkable line in the ledger is the one that says no ledger was demanded. The Reformed Gill states the fact: the king's secretary and the high priest 'never called to account the overseers of the workmen, to whom they committed the money to pay them with; never examined how they laid it out, and for what.' The grammatical-historical Ellicott refines it: read 'and they were not wont to reckon' — the trust was habitual, not a single indulgence; and 'the doers of the work' here means the artisans, 'not the superintendents, as in v. 11.' Nor is the notice 'a covert thrust at the priests, as Thenius imagines': the statement of the verse recurs at the restoration under Josiah (2 Kings 22:7), 'where the priests are not concerned in the matter at all.' All that is meant is that the officials entrusted with the oversight of the work 'were above suspicion, and did not belie their reputation.'

The Wesleyan witnesses set the fact in its experience. Clarke: 'They placed great confidence in them, and were not disappointed, for they dealt faithfully.' Wesley adds that the confidence was earned, not presumed — 'for they perceived by many experiments that they were faithful.' The puritan Matthew Henry crowns the verse: such a reputation had these men gotten for honesty 'that there was no occasion to examine their bills or audit their accounts.' And he presses the lesson far beyond the temple precincts: 'Let all that are entrusted with public money, or public work, learn hence to deal faithfully, as those that know God will reckon with them, whether men do or no. Those that think it is no sin to cheat the government, cheat the country, or cheat the church, will be of another mind when God shall set their sins in order before them.'

The verse completes the chapter's quiet argument about trust. The first arrangement failed for want of accountability; the second flourished until accountability itself became unnecessary — the chest and the counting and the weighed bags built a reputation that finally made auditing superfluous. Faithfulness proved 'by many experiments,' as Wesley says, is the one audit that discharges every other; and the witnesses are careful to observe that this liberty was not the beginning of the system but its reward. Men who had been watched well became men who no longer needed watching, and God's house was built by hands that could be left alone with the silver.
16:
One stream of revenue is expressly left outside the chest, and the witnesses use it to trace the border between God's house and God's servants. The Reformed Gill explains the money as most have understood it: this was what 'persons at a distance sent for their trespass and sin offerings instead of cattle,' with which the sacrifices were bought; and what remained of it was not carried into the temple fund nor used in the repairs. The grammatical-historical Keil states the law with exactness on one side and dissents sharply on the other. For the trespass-offering the law itself gave money to the priests — compensation according to the priest's valuation, with a fifth part added (Lev. 5:16; Num. 5:9), and that whether the wrong was against Jehovah or against a neighbour who had died before restitution. But of sin-money the law knows nothing: 'in the case of the sin-offerings the priests received no money according to the law'; and of the assumption which 'most of the commentators' make — that those who lived at a distance sent money for sin-offerings and the priests retained what was over — 'there is not the slightest trace of any such custom.' Ellicott keeps to the administrative sense: trespass money and sin money (Lev. 5:15-18; Num. 5:8) 'was not wont to be brought' into the chest but 'continued to accrue' to the priests; 'the general sense is that the priests were not deprived of their lawful revenues by the new arrangement,' whose real novelty was this — 'that henceforth gifts intended for the sanctuary itself were kept apart from the gifts intended for the priesthood.'

The puritan Matthew Henry gives the exemption its charter. While they were getting all they could for the repair of the temple, 'they did not break in upon that which was the stated maintenance of the priests'; the trespass-money and the sin-money given them by the law (Lev. 5:15-16) 'were reserved to them. Let not the servants of the temple be starved, under colour of repairing the breaches of it.'

So the witnesses record a real difference over what precisely the sin-money was — the offering-money of distant worshippers with its surplus, as Gill and most suppose, or a category Keil insists the law never created — while agreeing entirely on what the verse is for. The zeal that took the building fund from the priests did not take their bread; reform stripped them of a trust they had failed, not of the portion God had assigned them. A reformation that starves its ministers, the witnesses imply, has only exchanged one breach in God's house for another.
17:
The chapter turns without warning from building to invasion, and the witnesses supply the hinge the text leaves silent. The Reformed Gill fixes the moment theologically: this was 'when Jehoiada was dead, and Jehoash was become an apostate'; then 'the Lord suffered the king of Syria to be a scourge to him.' Gath, formerly one of the Philistine principalities, had been subdued by David and remained in Israelite hands ever since; Hazael began with it 'as nearest to him, to open the way for what he had further in view.' The Wesleyan Clarke traces the same title-deeds (2 Sam. 8:1; 1 Chron. 18:1) and shrugs at the pretext: 'On what pretence Hazael seized it, we cannot tell; he had the ultima ratio regum, power to do it, and he wanted more territory.'

The grammatical-historical Ellicott builds the historical frame. 'Then' is 'at that time': the invasion followed Hazael's successes against Jehoahaz, who came to the northern throne 'in the very year when Jehoash took in hand the restoration of the Temple' (compare v. 6 with 2 Kings 13:1; 2 Kings 13:3); Gath at this time 'either belonged to, or was in league with, Judah,' Rehoboam having included it in his system of national defences (2 Chron. 11:8), and was perhaps the capital's only important outpost on the western side. He notes Ewald's conjecture that the petty Philistine states had invited Hazael's intervention against their suzerain; and he observes that a century later Gaza, Ashdod, Ascalon, and Ekron — but not Gath — appear as Philistine kingdoms in the annals of Sennacherib and Esarhaddon, agreeing with the record that Uzziah broke down the wall of Gath (2 Chron. 26:6). 'Set his face' — that is, determined (compare Luke 9:51).

Keil binds the two biblical accounts into one campaign: Hazael had gone down along the coast after defeating Israel, took Gath, and then determined to advance on Jerusalem; according to 2 Chron. 24:23-24 he sent an army which 'destroyed all the princes of the nation,' a small Syrian force smiting the very large army of Judah. 'In this way the two brief accounts of the war may be both reconciled and explained' — against the opinion 'still repeated by Thenius' that they cannot be squared. The puritan Matthew Henry names the deepest cause of the calamity: Hazael threatened 'Jerusalem, the royal city, the holy city, but whose defence, on account of its sinfulness, had departed.' The king who had mended the sanctuary had broken faith with its Lord, and the mended walls could not save him.
18:
Jerusalem is saved by being emptied, and the witnesses count what the deliverance cost. The Reformed Gill notes what went: the hallowed things that Jehoshaphat, Jehoram, and Ahaziah had dedicated 'to sacred uses, and had laid up in the temple,' together with the king's own gifts and all the gold of both treasuries. The grammatical-historical Ellicott pauses over the strange donors: Jehoram and Ahaziah, though they had sought to naturalise the Baal-worship, 'had not ventured to abolish that of Jehovah,' and had 'even tried to conciliate the powerful priesthood and numerous adherents of the national religion, by dedicating gifts to the sanctuary.' Nor is it a wonder that so much treasure remained after the repair-fund years, for the temple treasure 'appears to have been regarded as a reserve, only to be touched in case of grave national emergency like the present.' 'He went away from Jerusalem' means Hazael 'withdrew his forces' — it is not said that he came in person; and against Thenius, who would sever this expedition from that of 2 Chron. 24:23, Ellicott holds that 'the two narratives thus supplement each other' — the defeat of Judah's army in Chronicles accounts for the sacrifice of the treasures here, and the bribe here explains the Syrians' withdrawal there.

The puritan Matthew Henry audits the transaction in four entries. Granting that, if it were lawful for the public safety, it was 'better part with the gold of the temple than expose the temple itself,' yet: had Joash not forsaken God and forfeited his protection, 'he might have forced Hazael to retire'; he diminished himself, losing 'the honour of a prince and a soldier, and of an Israelite too, in alienating the dedicated things'; he impoverished himself and his kingdom; and he 'tempted Hazael to come again' for so cheap a booty. 'And it had this effect,' Henry adds, 'for the next year the host of Syria came up against Jerusalem, destroyed the prince, and plundered the city' (2 Chron. 24:23-24). The Wesleyan Clarke draws the same grim sequel: 'He dearly bought a peace which was of short duration'; when Hazael returned, Jehoash, 'having no more treasures, was obliged to hazard a battle, which he lost, with the principal part of his nobility, so that Judah was totally ruined, and Jehoash shortly after slain in his bed by his own servants.'

The irony is not lost on any of them: the king who spent his best years filling the house of the Lord strips it bare in a day. The repairer of the breaches becomes the plunderer of his own repairs — a parable, the witnesses suggest, of what apostasy always does with the devotions of a better past.
19:
The annalist closes the reign with his customary formula, and the witnesses let its very reticence speak. The Reformed Gill identifies the register: the rest of the acts of Jehoash were written 'in the registers and annals of those princes,' the book of the chronicles of the kings of Judah — and some of them, he notes, may still be met with in the canonical books of the Chronicles. What those further acts contained, the Wesleyan Clarke has no doubt: 'We have already seen that this man, so promising in the beginning of his reign, apostatized, became an idolater, encouraged idolatry among his subjects, and put the high priest Zechariah, the son of Jehoiada his benefactor, to death; and now God visited that blood upon him by the hands of the tyrannous king of Syria, and by his own servants.' The bare 'are they not written' thus covers, on Clarke's reading, the darkest matter of the story — the apostasy after Jehoiada's death and the martyrdom in the temple court which Kings passes over in silence and Chronicles records at length.

The puritan Matthew Henry, surveying the same close, turns the record into a mirror: 'Let us review the character of Jehoash, and consider what we may learn from it. When we see what a sad conclusion there was to so promising a beginning, it ought to make us seek into our spiritual declinings.' The remedy he prescribes is not resolution but Christ: 'If we know any thing of Christ as the foundation of our faith and hope, let us desire to know nothing but Christ' — praying that the work of the blessed Spirit on our souls be manifest, and that we be brought over 'from dead works to serve the living and true God.' For Henry the moral of the epitaph is the peril of a borrowed religion, sustained by another man's influence and dying with it; Joash 'began in the spirit and ended in the flesh,' and God 'usually sets marks of his displeasure upon apostates, even in this life.'

Between them the witnesses catch the strange doubleness of the king's memorial. The book of Kings preserves chiefly what Joash did for the house of God — the chest, the fund, the mended breaches; the fuller annals, and Chronicles after them, preserve what he did against it. His epitaph is thus divided like his life, and the formula that sends the reader elsewhere for 'the rest of the acts' is, in this case, no idle convention: the rest of the acts are the tragedy, and the witnesses will not let the reader close the reign without knowing where it is written.
20:
The end comes in a single sentence, and the witnesses supply both its history and its riddle. The Reformed Gill sets the sequence from Chronicles: the conspiracy was formed 'after he had slain Zechariah the son of Jehoiada, who reproved him for his idolatry; and after a second expedition of the king of Syria,' who came to Jerusalem, spoiled it, and left Jehoash diseased. The grammatical-historical Keil agrees: not long after the departure of the Syrians, who had left the king, according to 2 Chron. 24:25, 'with many wounds,' his servants formed a conspiracy and slew him upon his bed. Ellicott adds that 'his servants' are 'his immediate attendants' (compare 2 Kings 8:15) — the assassins of a sick-room, not an army.

Where he died, the witnesses cannot agree, for the text itself is dark. The Wesleyan Clarke is confident: the house of Millo 'was a royal palace, built by David' (2 Sam. 5:9), situated between the old city of Jerusalem and the city of David, and Silla 'is supposed to be the name of the road or causeway that led to it.' The grammatical-historical commentators refuse the confidence. For Keil the description of the locality 'is perfectly obscure for us,' and the conjecture that Beth-Millo is the famous castle of Millo is 'precluded' by the fact that the castle is always called 'the Millo,' with the article. Ellicott concurs that 'the precise locality cannot be determined': Thenius supposes the sorely wounded king had retired for greater safety into 'the castle palace,' Ewald that he was murdered 'while engaged in the fortress,' the chronicler that he was murdered in his bed; as for Silla, the name is otherwise unknown, and 'the text is probably corrupt, for Silla is almost exactly like Millo in Hebrew writing' — the Vatican Septuagint omits 'which goeth down' altogether.

The Wesleyan Wesley and the puritan Henry read the providence in the crime. Wesley: Chronicles tells us that 'his murdering the prophet, Jehoiada's son, was the provocation. In this, how unrighteous so ever they were, yet the Lord was righteous'; and this was not the only time God let kings know 'it was at their peril, if they touched his anointed, or did his prophets any harm. Thus fell Joash, who began in the spirit, and ended in the flesh.' Henry observes that the conspirators aimed not at the crown — 'they opposed not his son's succeeding him' — but at revenge for the prophet's blood; vengeance was not theirs to take, and yet in it God was righteous, 'for when he comes to make inquisition for blood, the blood of prophets will run the account very high.' The murdered prophet had a mightier avenger than the men who held the knives.
21:
The last verse names the assassins, and the witnesses find a judgment written even in the names. The Reformed Gill notes the variants: the man called Jozachar here appears in Chronicles as Zabad, and his mother Shimeath is said to be 'an Ammonitess.' The Wesleyan Clarke completes the pair from 2 Chron. 24:26: Jehozabad was the son not of Shomer but of Shimrith, 'a Moabitess' — the avengers of the temple's martyr were sons of Ammon and Moab. The grammatical-historical Ellicott corrects the connective — read 'And Jozachar... it was that smote him' — and preserves from Thenius a 'curious coincidence': the dying Zechariah had cried, 'Jehovah see, and avenge!' — and the prophet was avenged by Jozachar, 'Jehovah remembers,' the son of Shimeath, 'hearing,' and by Jehozabad, 'Jehovah bestows,' the son of Shomer, 'watcher.' The very roll of the murderers reads like the answer to the martyr's prayer.

The burial carries the people's own verdict. Joash was buried with his fathers in the city of David — but, Clarke notes, 'they did not bury him in the sepulchres of the kings; this is supposed to express the popular disapprobation of his conduct.' Ellicott reads the clause the same way: 'with his fathers' means in the city of David, yet not in the royal tombs (2 Chron. 24:25). With that half-honoured grave Clarke pronounces the epitaph: 'Thus ended a reign full of promise and hope in the beginning, but profligate, cruel, and ruinous in the end. Never was the hand of God's justice more signally stretched out against an apostate king and faithless people.' Hazael had 'a plenary commission'; a small company of Syrians overthrew the mighty armies of Judah 'because they had forsaken the Lord God' (2 Chron. 24:24). 'As righteousness exalteth a nation, so sin is the disgrace and confusion of any people... Sin destroys both counsel and strength; and the wicked flee when none pursue.'

And yet the verse does not end in the grave: 'Amaziah his son reigned in his stead.' The conspirators, as Henry observed on the previous verse, never sought to bar the succession, and so the line preserved through Athaliah's massacre passes intact through a second regicide. The chapter that began with a boy brought out of the temple's hiding-place ends with his son quietly seated on his throne; the king failed, and fell, and was refused the tombs of his fathers, but the lamp of David — trimmed in that same temple forty years before — burns on past his dishonoured grave toward the Son of David in whom it can never be put out.
